summary:
The University of Oxford (c. 1096–1167 AD, formally organized) developed as an institution of higher learning in the English town of Oxford through a gradual accumulation of masters and scholars teaching there from the late 11th century, crystallizing into a recognized studium generale around 1167 when King Henry II banned English scholars from attending the [[event:2266|University of Paris]] — driving a concentration of academic talent to Oxford. The university became the oldest English-speaking university and the model for British higher education, including Cambridge (founded 1209 by masters fleeing a dispute at Oxford).

Oxford's institutional model — the university of masters rather than students, organized around residential colleges that combined living, dining, and teaching — differed from the Bologna model and became the pattern for English and later American elite education. The colleges (beginning with Merton, Balliol, and University colleges in the 1240s–1260s) created self-governing scholarly communities with their own endowments, chapels, and academic cultures. Oxford became an early center of natural philosophy and empirical inquiry: [[event:3618|Roger Bacon]] studied and taught there in the 13th century, and the 'Oxford Calculators' of Merton College in the 14th century developed early mathematical treatments of motion and change that anticipated Galileo's kinematics. Oxford's theological controversies — including the teachings of John Wycliffe (c. 1330–1384), whose followers were the Lollards and who produced the first complete English Bible — made it a center of the intellectual ferment that preceded the Protestant Reformation. Today Oxford and Cambridge together represent one of the most influential educational models in world history, their graduates disproportionately shaping British and global intellectual, political, and cultural life.
